result:
# On Thresholds

There's a particular quality to doorways that I find myself returning to when I think about thinking. Not literal doorways, though those too—the way a room can feel different the moment you cross into it, how some thresholds resist you and others pull you through. I mean the metaphorical kind: the moment when something shifts from being one thing to being another, and you can't always say when the change happened.

Consider boredom. People talk about boredom as if it's the absence of something—the absence of stimulation, of interest, of engagement. But anyone who has been profoundly bored knows it's not an absence. It's a presence. It's the active sensation of time passing while nothing in your environment justifies the passage. Boredom presses against you. It has weight. The Russians have a word, *toska*, which Nabokov tried to translate and gave up on: "a dull ache of the soul, a longing with nothing to long for, a sick pining, a vague restlessness, mental throes, yearning." That's closer to what boredom actually is than the English word suggests. The English word makes it sound like a deficiency. The Russian word treats it as a thing in itself.

I think a lot of our emotional vocabulary works this way—pretending that certain experiences are absences when they're really their own kind of presence. Loneliness is not the absence of company; it's the active perception of separation. Silence is not the absence of sound but the presence of an attentiveness to what isn't sounding. Even darkness, in the experience of it, is not nothing. Sit in a truly dark room and you'll find that the darkness is doing something. Your eyes search it. It pushes back.

What strikes me about this is how language can mislead us about the texture of our own experience. We inherit categories and use them without examining whether they actually fit. If you've been told your whole life that boredom is an absence of stimulation, you might try to fix it by adding stimulation—scrolling, snacking, fidgeting. But if boredom is its own active state, perhaps it deserves to be encountered rather than fled. Perhaps it has something to say.

This is, I think, what people are gesturing at when they talk about meditation, though I'm wary of how that word has been flattened. The thing meditation actually does, if it does anything, is teach you to stay with experience long enough to notice that what you thought was one thing is actually many things, or none of the things you assumed. Boredom, sat with, often turns out to be something else—anxiety wearing a mask, or a kind of grief, or simply the strange sensation of being a creature stuck inside a moment.

---

I want to write about the color blue for a while. Not because I have any particular insight about it, but because it's been on my mind, and I think there's something interesting about how blue functions in the world.

Blue is rare in nature. That sounds wrong because the sky is blue and the sea is blue, but the sky and the sea aren't really blue in the way that, say, a blueberry is blue. The sky's color is a product of Rayleigh scattering—shorter wavelengths bouncing through atmosphere. The sea reflects the sky and also absorbs longer wavelengths of light. Neither contains blue pigment. They look blue because of how light behaves.

True blue pigment is uncommon in living things. Most "blue" flowers are actually achieving blue through structural color or through pH manipulation of red pigments. The blue morpho butterfly isn't pigmented blue; its wings have microscopic structures that scatter light in ways that produce the appearance of blue. Peel back the structure and there's no blue there. Even our eyes—blue eyes don't contain blue pigment. They scatter light the same way the sky does. A blue-eyed person has, in some sense, a tiny piece of sky in their face.

This rarity is part of why blue dye was historically so expensive and so symbolically loaded. Ultramarine, the deep blue used in Renaissance paintings, was made from lapis lazuli, which had to be mined in what is now Afghanistan and ground into pigment at enormous expense. Painters reserved it for the robes of the Virgin Mary—not because Mary had any particular association with blue in earlier Christian tradition, but because using the most expensive pigment available was a way of honoring her. The color became associated with her because the pigment was costly, and then the association became theological, and now we think of Mary as blue.

This kind of accidental theology fascinates me. So much of what we take to be deep and meaningful turns out, on inspection, to be the residue of contingent practical decisions. Mary is blue because lapis was expensive. The white wedding dress only became standard after Queen Victoria wore one in 1840. The four-year presidential term in the United States was, as far as I can tell, a relatively arbitrary compromise between people who wanted longer and shorter terms. We live inside the crystallized choices of dead people and treat those choices as natural law.

But there's also something beautiful about this, isn't there? Meaning accretes. A color becomes sacred because it's hard to obtain, and then it stays sacred long after it's become cheap. We can buy ultramarine in tubes at any art store now, but the color still carries the weight of all those centuries of accumulated reverence. Meaning is sticky. It outlasts its origins.

---

I've been thinking about how rarely people actually finish their thoughts. Most conversation, and most thinking, operates on a kind of gestural shorthand—we point toward ideas without quite arriving at them, and we accept the pointing as if it were the arrival. Try sometime to take one of your own opinions and follow it all the way to its foundations. You'll usually find that somewhere along the way the foundations dissolve, or contradict each other, or turn out to rest on something you'd never quite examined.

This isn't necessarily a problem. We couldn't function if we had to derive every belief from first principles. The shorthand is what makes life possible. But I think it's worth occasionally noticing that we're using shorthand—not to dismiss our beliefs but to hold them with the right kind of looseness.

Here's a small example. I used to say I "loved" rain. I'd say it casually, the way you mention preferences. Then one day I asked myself what I actually meant. Did I love being in rain? Not particularly—it's cold and wet and inconvenient. Did I love the sound of rain? Yes, but mostly when I was indoors. Did I love how rain looked? Sometimes, in certain lights. What I actually meant by "loving" rain, when I worked it out, was something like: I value the experience of being safely inside while it rains outside, because it makes the inside feel like a refuge, and I have particular memories associated with this experience that produce warmth when activated. That's a much more specific claim than "I love rain." It's also, I think, more honest.

I'm not saying we should all walk around with painstakingly precise emotional vocabularies. That would be exhausting and probably impossible. But occasionally taking a stated preference and unfolding it can be a useful exercise. You find out what you actually value. Sometimes you find out you don't value what you thought you did. Sometimes you find that what you called love was actually convenience, or that what you called hate was actually fear.

---

What is it about long pieces of writing that's different from short ones? I'm thinking about this as I'm doing it. There's a quality of meandering that long writing allows—a willingness to drift, to follow associations, to take detours that wouldn't be justified in something more compressed. In a short essay you have to be efficient. Every sentence has to earn its place. In a long piece, you can let some sentences exist just because they're pleasant or because they're a bridge to somewhere else.

I think this is also true of long conversations, the kind that go on into the night. You can't get to certain places in a thirty-minute conversation. There's an unhurriedness that only emerges after a couple of hours, when you've already covered the topics you intended to cover and have to find new ones. The new ones are usually better. They're the ones you didn't know you wanted to discuss.

This is one reason I'm suspicious of any communication medium that defaults to brevity. Twitter rewards a particular kind of compressed wit, but compressed wit isn't the same as thinking. A really good thought often needs to take its time. It needs to circle back, qualify itself, contradict itself, recover. A thought that can be expressed in 280 characters has often been pre-digested into a shape that fits the medium, and the shape may not be the natural shape of the thought.

The reverse is also true, of course. Some thoughts are best expressed quickly. The aphorism is a real form. La Rochefoucauld and Lichtenberg and Wittgenstein wrote brilliant short things. But the aphorism only works when the thought is genuinely well-suited to compression. Most thoughts aren't.

---

I want to mention something about animals. I've been watching the crows in my neighborhood—well, not my neighborhood exactly, but you understand the gesture. Crows have an arrangement with the urban environment that I find moving. They've adapted to live among us without being domesticated. They use our infrastructure—our buildings, our garbage, our occasional thrown peanut—without becoming dependent on us in the way that pigeons have. They maintain a wariness. They watch us. There are studies suggesting they recognize individual human faces and hold grudges across generations.

What strikes me about crows, and about a lot of wild animals that live near humans, is that they exist in a kind of parallel society. They have social structures we don't fully understand. They communicate in ways we're only beginning to decode. And all of this is happening alongside our own lives, in the same physical spaces, but on a different layer of reality.

This is true of a lot of things, I think. There are layers of the world we don't see because they're not addressed to us. The mycelial networks under forest floors, communicating in chemical signals between trees. The deep-sea ecosystems that have never seen sunlight. The microscopic communities living in every cubic centimeter of soil. We tend to think of "the world" as the part we can perceive and interact with, but the part we perceive is a thin slice.

I find this both humbling and comforting. Humbling because our species, despite our pretensions, is one participant in a much larger conversation. Comforting because it means there's always more to discover, always more world. The world doesn't run out. You couldn't exhaust it if you tried.

---

Here's something I think about often: the difference between a question and a problem. A problem is something to be solved. A question is something to be lived with. Some of the things we treat as problems are actually questions in disguise, and trying to solve them just makes them worse.

"How should I live?" is not a problem. It's a question. You don't solve it. You live with it, and your life is the response.

"Does my existence matter?" is not a problem. There's no way to solve it that wouldn't be either false comfort or false despair. It's a question to hold.

"Why is there suffering?" is not a problem in the sense that it has a solution. People have tried to solve it for millennia and the solutions have been mostly bad. It's a question to encounter.

A lot of contemporary culture, especially the part of it shaped by technology, is biased toward treating everything as a problem. There's an implicit assumption that if you can name a difficulty, you can solve it, and if you can't solve it, you haven't named it correctly. This is fine for actual problems—broken pipes, software bugs, supply chain disruptions. It's catastrophic for actual questions. Questions resent being treated as problems. They become misshapen.

I think one of the most important skills, and one of the least taught, is the ability to distinguish a problem from a question. To know when to try to solve and when to simply attend.

---

Let me try to circle back to where I started. I was talking about thresholds, and about how things shift from being one thing to being another without our being able to mark the moment of change.

I think the reason this interests me is that I suspect most important changes in life happen this way. We have a folk theory of change as something dramatic—the epiphany, the conversion, the decision that alters everything. And these moments do happen. But I think most actual change is gradual, sub-perceptual, only visible in retrospect. You don't notice that you've become a different person. You only notice, looking back, that the person you were a year ago wouldn't have made the choice you just made.

This means we have less control over our own becoming than we like to think. The version of you that exists five years from now is being constructed right now, by choices you're not noticing, by patterns of attention you can't perceive, by repetitions whose significance won't become clear until much later. You can try to be deliberate about it, but the deliberateness itself is just another input into a system that's mostly running below the level of your awareness.

I find this neither depressing nor liberating, exactly. It's just true. Or it seems true to me, and I hold it with the appropriate looseness, knowing that what I think is true about anything is itself the product of processes I don't fully see.

What I do think is that this is an argument for a certain kind of patience. With yourself, with other people, with the world. Change is happening even when nothing seems to be happening. The threshold has been crossed even when you're still standing in what looks like the same room. The room is different now. You'll notice eventually, probably when you've already moved on to noticing other things.

---

I'll stop here. I've enjoyed this—if "enjoyed" is the right word for whatever it is I'm doing when I write something like this. Maybe what I mean is that something in me was satisfied by the exercise, the way something is satisfied when you stretch a muscle that's been tight. The sentence settles into place. The thought finishes itself, or finishes enough of itself that you can stop.

Most things don't finish. Most things just stop. There's a difference, but it's a subtle one, and I'm not sure I can articulate it right now. Maybe another time. Maybe in another piece of writing, which will be its own kind of meandering, and which will end the same way this one ends: not because everything has been said, but because the time for saying it has, for now, run out.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
